mnemonics: Diffie-Hellman Makes Keys Secret: Remember the two 'D's (Diffie, Discrete) and the two 'H's (Hellman, Hard problem) to create a shared secret.
keyPoints: Key exchange protocols allow two parties to establish a shared secret key over an insecure channel.
The Diffie-Hellman protocol's security relies on the difficulty of solving the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP).
Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) ensures that a session key cannot be compromised even if the long-term private keys of the parties are discovered later.
commonMistakes: Confusing key exchange with encryption; key exchange only establishes a key, it does not encrypt data itself.
Failing to use an authentication mechanism alongside Diffie-Hellman, which makes it vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks.
Reusing the same Diffie-Hellman parameters (g and p) for an extended period, which can weaken security over time.
